description: HLLOP - LIFE 06 QZ - Group Life Insurance - Section 3
examTips: Focus on coverage classes, underlying insurance products, sales tax, dismemberment benefits, and exclusions in AD&D coverage.

questions.question: An employer's group life plan provides a $50,000 flat coverage rate for hourly workers and an earnings multiple of two times salary for executives. Can the employer legally structure the master contract this way?
options: A. No, it is illegal to offer different benefit levels.
B. No, executives must be in a completely separate master contract.
C. Yes, a master contract can establish different membership classes, provided coverage is calculated the same way for everyone within a specific class.
D. Yes, provided the hourly workers agree in writing.
optionFeedback: Different benefit levels for different classes are allowed under one master contract.
Different classes do not require separate master contracts.
Yes, a master contract can establish different membership classes, provided coverage is calculated the same way for everyone within a specific class.
Hourly worker consent is not required to establish different classes.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify that this question concerns whether a single master contract can have different benefit structures.
Step 2: Apply the LLQP principle that master contracts can establish multiple membership classes with different benefit formulas.
Step 3: Recognize that within each class, coverage must be calculated consistently for all members.
Step 4: Eliminate distractors - different levels are allowed; separate contracts are not required; consent is not required.
Step 5: Select the best answer. Option C: Yes, a master contract can establish different membership classes, provided coverage is calculated the same way for everyone within a specific class.
questions.kidExplanation: A boss is allowed to have different rules for different teams. They can give the managers one type of insurance and the hourly workers a different type, as long as everyone on the same team gets the same deal!

questions.question: Which of the following is a key characteristic that distinguishes basic group life insurance from individual term life insurance?
options: A. Group life insurance always builds a cash surrender value.
B. The individual plan member does not hold the insurance contract; the employer holds the master contract.
C. Group life insurance death benefits are fully taxable.
D. Group life insurance requires a medical exam for everyone.
optionFeedback: Group term insurance does not build cash value.
The individual plan member does not hold the insurance contract; the employer holds the master contract.
Group life insurance death benefits are tax-free to beneficiaries, just like individual insurance.
Group insurance does not require individual medical exams.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify that this question concerns a fundamental structural difference between group and individual insurance.
Step 2: Apply the LLQP principle that in group insurance, the employer holds the master contract, and employees hold certificates of insurance.
Step 3: Recognize that employees do not own their group policies - they participate in the employer's group contract.
Step 4: Eliminate distractors - group insurance can be term (no cash value); benefits are tax-free; no individual medical exams are required.
Step 5: Select the best answer. Option B: The individual plan member does not hold the insurance contract; the employer holds the master contract.
questions.kidExplanation: With group insurance, you don't own the contract—your boss does! You just get a little booklet that says you are allowed to be a part of the boss's big plan.

questions.question: Greg resigns and has 31 days to exercise his conversion privilege. Tragically, Greg dies in a car accident 15 days after leaving his job, before he had applied for the conversion policy. What will happen?
options: A. The death benefit is forfeited because he was not an active employee.
B. The death benefit will be paid out because the group coverage remains in force during the 31-day conversion period.
C. Only 50% of the death benefit will be paid.
D. His beneficiary must pay the premium retroactively.
optionFeedback: Group coverage does not end immediately upon resignation.
The death benefit will be paid out because the group coverage remains in force during the 31-day conversion period.
The full benefit is paid if the death occurs during the conversion period.
No retroactive premium is required.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify that this question concerns the continuation of coverage during the conversion period.
Step 2: Apply the LLQP principle that group coverage remains in force for 31 days following termination, even if the employee has not yet converted.
Step 3: Recognize that the conversion period provides a safety period where benefits remain active.
Step 4: Evaluate - Greg's death occurred within the 31-day window while coverage was still active.
Step 5: Select the best answer. Option B: The death benefit will be paid out because the group coverage remains in force during the 31-day conversion period.
questions.kidExplanation: The 31 days after you leave your job is a magical safety bubble. If something bad happens to you while you are inside that 31-day bubble, your old insurance still works and pays your family!

questions.question: An employee with $100,000 in AD&D coverage loses a single limb in an accident. How is the accidental dismemberment benefit typically calculated and paid?
options: A. As a monthly disability income payment.
B. As a single lump-sum payment representing a specific percentage of the base AD&D amount, based on a schedule of loss.
C. The full $100,000 base amount is paid out immediately.
D. It only reimburses out-of-pocket medical expenses.
optionFeedback: Dismemberment benefits are lump sum, not monthly disability income.
As a single lump-sum payment representing a specific percentage of the base AD&D amount, based on a schedule of loss.
A lost limb does not trigger the full base amount; it is a percentage based on the schedule.
AD&D benefits reimburse the loss itself, not medical expenses.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify that this question concerns how dismemberment benefits are calculated under AD&D.
Step 2: Apply the LLQP principle that AD&D policies include a "schedule of loss" that specifies what percentage of the base benefit is paid for different types of losses.
Step 3: Recognize that loss of a limb typically pays a percentage (often 50%) of the base amount, not the full amount.
Step 4: Understand that benefits are paid as a lump sum, not monthly or for medical expenses.
Step 5: Select the best answer. Option B: As a single lump-sum payment representing a specific percentage of the base AD&D amount, based on a schedule of loss.
questions.kidExplanation: If you lose an arm or a leg in an accident, the insurance company looks at a special chart. The chart tells them exactly what percentage of the big money pile to give you to help you out.

questions.question: According to standard CLHIA guidelines in most of Canada, what is the maximum amount of group life insurance a departing employee can typically convert to an individual policy without evidence of insurability?
options: D. The full face amount is always convertible.
optionFeedback: $50,000 is below the standard maximum.
$100,000 is below the standard maximum.
$200,000 is the standard maximum under CLHIA guidelines.
CLHIA guidelines do establish a maximum limit.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify that this question concerns the CLHIA maximum for guaranteed-issue conversion.
Step 2: Apply the CLHIA guideline that employees can convert up to $200,000 without medical evidence.
Step 3: Recognize that if coverage exceeds $200,000, the excess would require medical underwriting.
Step 4: Understand this limit protects the insurer from excessive adverse selection.
Step 5: Select the best answer. Option C: $200,000
questions.kidExplanation: There is a cap on how much insurance you can take with you when you leave your job without taking a health test. For most of Canada, that maximum limit is $200,000. It's the biggest slice of pie you're allowed to take!

questions.question: Celine purchased group creditor life insurance through her bank to cover her $30,000 car loan. Two years later, Celine dies. At the time of her death, the loan balance is $18,000. Who receives the death benefit, and how much is paid?
options: A. Her estate receives $30,000.
B. The bank receives $18,000 to extinguish the debt.
C. Her named personal beneficiary receives $18,000.
D. The bank receives $30,000 and forwards $12,000 to her estate.
optionFeedback: Creditor insurance benefits are paid to the creditor, not the estate.
The bank receives $18,000 to extinguish the debt.
With creditor insurance, the beneficiary is the creditor, not the insured.
The benefit is only for the outstanding loan balance, not the original amount.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify that this question concerns how creditor life insurance works.
Step 2: Apply the principle that creditor insurance is designed to protect the creditor by paying off the outstanding loan balance.
Step 3: Recognize that the creditor (bank) is the beneficiary and receives the benefit.
Step 4: Calculate: Benefit equals the outstanding loan balance at death, which is $18,000.
Step 5: Select the best answer. Option B: The bank receives $18,000 to extinguish the debt.
questions.kidExplanation: If you borrow money to buy a car, and you pass away, "creditor" insurance pays the bank exactly what you still owe for the car. It protects your family so they don't have to pay off your car loan!

questions.question: ABC Manufacturing requires new hires to work for three months before they are eligible to join the group life insurance plan. What is this three-month timeframe called?
options: A. The conversion period
B. The contestability period
C. The probationary period (or waiting period)
D. The enrolment period
optionFeedback: The conversion period applies after termination, not upon hire.
The contestability period relates to claim investigations, not to new hire eligibility.
The probationary period (or waiting period) is the three-month wait for new hires.
The enrolment period is when an eligible employee enrolls in the plan.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify that this question asks for the terminology for the initial waiting period before coverage begins.
Step 2: Apply the LLQP definitions - the probationary period (also called the waiting period) is the time a new employee must work before becoming eligible for group insurance.
Step 3: Recognize the standard timeframes are typically 1-3 months depending on the plan.
Step 4: Eliminate distractors - conversion period is post-termination; contestability period is for claims; enrolment period is when eligible employees sign up.
Step 5: Select the best answer. Option C: The probationary period (or waiting period)
questions.kidExplanation: When you start a new job, you are the new kid on the block. The boss makes you wait a few months to prove you are going to stay before they give you all the cool insurance perks. This waiting time is called the probationary period!

questions.question: For a group life insurance plan established in Ontario, Quebec, or Manitoba, which of the following taxes is applied to the premiums paid?
options: A. Federal capital gains tax
B. Provincial retail sales tax (RST)
C. Goods and Services Tax (GST)
D. Municipal payroll tax
optionFeedback: Capital gains tax applies to investment gains, not insurance premiums.
Provincial retail sales tax (RST) is applied to insurance premiums in Ontario, Quebec, and Manitoba.
GST is a federal tax, but Insurance taxes are provincially regulated.
Municipal payroll taxes are not applied to group insurance premiums.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify that this question concerns the specific tax treatment of group insurance premiums in certain provinces.
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule that in Ontario, Quebec, and Manitoba, provincial retail sales tax (RST) applies to group insurance premiums.
Step 3: Note that other provinces may have different tax treatments (e.g., GST in some cases).
Step 4: Eliminate distractors - capital gains tax, GST as primary, and municipal taxes are not correct.
Step 5: Select the best answer. Option B: Provincial retail sales tax (RST)
questions.kidExplanation: In some places like Ontario, when you or your boss buy group insurance, the government adds a little extra shopping tax on top of the price, just like when you buy a toy at the store!

questions.question: Under most basic employer-sponsored group life insurance plans, the underlying coverage takes the form of which type of life insurance product?
options: A. Universal life insurance
B. Term-to-100 (T-100) insurance
C. Participating whole life insurance
D. Yearly renewable term (YRT) insurance
optionFeedback: Universal life is typically used for individual, not group plans.
T-100 is permanent insurance; group insurance uses renewable term.
Participating whole life is not the standard for group plans.
Yearly renewable term (YRT) is the standard underlying product for group insurance.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify that this question concerns what type of life insurance underlies group coverage.
Step 2: Apply the LLQP principle that group life insurance is typically provided through yearly renewable term (YRT) insurance.
Step 3: Recognize that YRT allows insurers to adjust premiums annually based on the group's age and claims experience.
Step 4: Eliminate distractors - universal, T-100, and permanent insurance are not standard for group plans.
Step 5: Select the best answer. Option D: Yearly renewable term (YRT) insurance
questions.kidExplanation: Group insurance is usually rented one year at a time. Every year, the insurance company checks how old the group is getting and gives the boss a brand new price tag for the next year!

questions.question: Under a typical Group Accidental Death and Dismemberment (AD&D) policy, which of the following scenarios would most likely result in a DENIED claim?
options: A. The employee dies as a passenger in a commercial airline crash.
B. The employee dies in a workplace accident caused by a forklift.
C. The employee dies in a car accident while legally intoxicated (impaired driving).
D. The employee loses his sight in an accidental chemical spill at home.
optionFeedback: Commercial airline deaths are typically covered as accidents.
Workplace accidents are covered under AD&D.
AD&D policies typically exclude deaths caused by the insured's illegal conduct (impaired driving).
Accidental loss of sight is typically covered under AD&D.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify that this question concerns exclusions in AD&D coverage.
Step 2: Apply the LLQP principle that AD&D policies typically exclude deaths or injuries caused by the insured's criminal conduct or illegal acts.
Step 3: Recognize that impaired driving is illegal in all jurisdictions and constitutes intentional violation of driving laws.
Step 4: Evaluate each option - airline crash and workplace accident are covered; chemical spill is accidental and covered; impaired driving is excluded.
Step 5: Select the best answer. Option C: The employee dies in a car accident while legally intoxicated (impaired driving).
questions.kidExplanation: The insurance company won't pay the extra "accident" money if the accident happened because you were doing something against the rules, like driving while drunk. You have to be safe and follow the rules to get the prize!
